---
title: Mandatory Assignment 1
author: GFS189, RTL959
date: today
date-format: MMMM DD, YYYY
execute: 
  echo: false
  warning: false
format:
  pdf: 
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    geometry:
      - top=20mm
      - left=20mm
      - bottom=20mm
      - right=20mm
    bibliography: references.bib
    cite-method: natbib
    biblio-style: apalike  
    lang: en
    fontsize: 12pt
    highlight-style: github-light
---


## Quarto - a framework for reproducible research

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>. When you click the **Preview** button in Positron or type `quarto render` in the terminal a .pdf document will be generated that includes both content and the output of embedded code. The template below can be used to submit your answers for the mandatory assignment. Within the template you find all typical issues. By default, code is omitted in the output (can be changed with `echo: true` in line 6). Formatting is done using markdown (e.g., check out how to use \# to define sections and subsections or how to generate bold text). Footnotes and citations are easy. You can also include math notation using LaTeX style. The template contains inline code execution (e.g. to automatically return the ticker of the asset with the highest Sharpe ratio) and shows how to embed figures into the document.

## Exercise

1. Download daily adjusted prices for all constituents of three stocks: AAPL, MSFT, and BA for the period from January 1st, 2000 until December 31st, 2025 from Yahoo!Finance. Explicitly include the code you use to download the data into your report. Compute the daily returns for each of the tickers.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import tidyfinance as tf
from plotnine import * 
import tidyfinance as tf
from mizani.formatters import percent_format
import io
import re
import zipfile
from curl_cffi import requests
import os 
from joblib import Parallel, delayed, cpu_count
from itertools import product
from dateutil.relativedelta import relativedelta
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import pyarrow.dataset as ds
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
from scipy.optimize import minimize
```

```{python} 
#| echo: true
#| cache: true
#| label: figures for period
start_date = "2000-01-01"
end_date = "2026-2-28"

sp500 = tf.download_data(
    domain="stock_prices",
    symbols="^GSPC",
    start_date=start_date,
    end_date=end_date,
)

tbill = tf.download_data(
    domain="stock_prices",
    symbols="^IRX",
    start_date=start_date,
    end_date=end_date,
)
sp500.head().round(3)

sp500_figure = (
    ggplot(sp500, aes(y="adjusted_close", x="date"))
    + geom_line()
    + labs(x="", y="", title="sp500 stock prices 2000-2026")
)
sp500_figure.show()

tbill_figure = (
    ggplot(tbill, aes(y="adjusted_close", x="date"))
    + geom_line()
    + labs(x="", y="", title="tbill 2000-2026")
)
tbill_figure.show()
```

1. Visualize the distribution of the daily returns.

```{python}
#| cache: true
#| label: returns
returns_sp500 = (sp500
    .sort_values("date")
    .assign(ret=lambda x: x["adjusted_close"].pct_change())
    .get(["symbol", "date", "ret"])
)
returns_sp500

returns_tbill = (tbill
    .sort_values("date")
    .assign(ret=lambda x: x["adjusted_close"].pct_change())
    .get(["symbol", "date", "ret"])
)
returns_tbill
```



```{python}
#| cache: true
#| label: monthly returns
returns_monthly_sp500 = (returns_sp500
    .assign(date=returns_sp500["date"].dt.to_period("M").dt.to_timestamp())
    .groupby(["symbol", "date"], as_index=False)
    .agg(ret=("ret", lambda x: np.prod(1 + x) - 1))
)
print(returns_monthly_sp500.head(100))

returns_monthly_tbill = (
    returns_tbill
    .assign(
        date=lambda x: x["date"].dt.to_period("M").dt.to_timestamp()
    )
    .groupby(["symbol", "date"], as_index=False)
    .agg(ret=("ret", "last"))   
)

returns_monthly_tbill["risk_free"] = (
    (1 + returns_monthly_tbill["ret"] / 100) ** (1/12) - 1
)

returns_monthly_tbill = returns_monthly_tbill.drop(columns="ret")


monthly_ret= (returns_monthly_tbill.merge(returns_monthly_sp500, how="inner", on="date")
    .assign(ret_excess=lambda x: x["ret"]-x["risk_free"])
)
monthly_ret.head(10)

```

```{python}
mu_m = monthly_ret["ret_excess"].mean()
sigma2_m = monthly_ret["ret_excess"].var()

print("Mean:", mu_m)
print("Variance:", sigma2_m)

annual_mean = 12 * monthly_ret["ret_excess"].mean()
annual_variance = 12 * monthly_ret["ret_excess"].var()

print(f"Monthly mean excess return:       {mu_m:.6f}")
print(f"Monthly variance:                 {sigma2_m:.6f}")
print(f"Annualized mean excess return:    {annual_mean:.6f}")
print(f"Annualized variance:              {annual_variance:.6f}")
```





The conclusion is the following:

## References
::::{#refs}
::::
